h1Title: Alcoholic Polyneuropathy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Alcoholic polyneuropathy is peripheral nerve damage caused by chronic heavy alcohol consumption, affecting an estimated 25-66% of people with chronic alcohol use disorder worldwide. The neuropathy results from a combination of direct ethanol neurotoxicity and nutritional deficiencies — particularly thiamine (vitamin B1) — that impair axonal transport and myelin maintenance. It typically presents insidiously with symmetric distal burning pain, numbness, and tingling beginning in the feet, progressing to weakness of the ankles and legs over months to years. When thiamine deficiency coexists, onset can be more acute and severe. Abstinence from alcohol is the single most effective intervention; nutritional supplementation and pain management support recovery, which is slow and often incomplete in advanced disease.

keyStats.prevalence: 25-66% of people with chronic alcohol use disorder develop clinically detectable polyneuropathy; subclinical nerve abnormalities on electrophysiology exceed 80% in heavy drinkers (Chopra & Twari 2012)
keyStats.demographics: Equal sex distribution in alcohol-related neuropathy; men have higher prevalence of alcohol use disorder overall; symptoms emerge after 10-20 years of heavy drinking in most patients
keyStats.avgAge: Typical age of presentation 40-60 years, reflecting years of cumulative alcohol exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 7-10% of the general adult population in high-income countries drinks at levels associated with neuropathy risk; globally alcohol use disorder affects approximately 107 million people (GBD 2020)

definition: Alcoholic polyneuropathy (ICD-10: G62.1) is a length-dependent peripheral neuropathy caused primarily by the toxic effects of ethanol and its metabolites (acetaldehyde) on peripheral nerve axons, compounded by co-existing nutritional deficiencies — most critically thiamine, folate, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 — in people with chronic heavy alcohol use. Unlike many toxic neuropathies, alcoholic polyneuropathy simultaneously impairs both small-caliber C and A-delta fibers (causing burning pain, thermal sensory loss, and autonomic dysfunction) and larger myelinated A-beta fibers (causing proprioceptive loss, vibration impairment, and eventually foot-drop and gait ataxia). Two subforms are recognized: a slowly progressive predominantly painful form driven by direct ethanol toxicity, and a more acute severe form driven by thiamine deficiency, overlapping with Wernicke-Korsakoff spectrum. Autonomic features — orthostatic hypotension, cardiac arrhythmia, gastroparesis, and erectile dysfunction — reflect concomitant small-fiber and autonomic nerve involvement. Neurology, addiction medicine, and internal medicine co-manage affected patients. Improvement requires abstinence from alcohol as the cornerstone, supplemented by nutritional rehabilitation.

typesClassification.description: Acute or subacute onset with predominantly motor deficits, proprioceptive loss, and ataxia; may overlap with Wernicke's encephalopathy. Associated with severe malnutrition. Responds more rapidly to thiamine replacement than the slow toxic form.

Insidious, length-dependent, predominantly painful sensory neuropathy in patients with adequate thiamine intake. Direct neurotoxicity from acetaldehyde and oxidative stress impairs axonal transport. This is the most common pattern in well-nourished heavy drinkers.

The most frequently encountered clinical form, combining both direct ethanol toxicity and variable degrees of B-vitamin deficiency. Severity correlates with cumulative alcohol dose and nutritional status.

primarySymptoms: Symmetric burning, aching, or electrical pain in the soles and toes — often described as 'walking on hot coals' or 'pins and needles' — typically worse at night and with touch (allodynia).
Numbness and reduced sensation beginning in the distal feet and ascending gradually up the legs in a stocking distribution, reflecting the length-dependent nature of axonal damage.
Vibration sense loss and impaired proprioception, causing gait unsteadiness, falls, and a wide-based stance in the dark (positive Romberg test).
Weakness of ankle dorsiflexion (foot-drop) in advanced neuropathy, producing a steppage gait and an increased risk of stumbling on uneven surfaces.
Muscle cramps and nocturnal leg cramps in the calves and feet, associated with electrolyte disturbances and axonal hyperexcitability.
Reduced or absent ankle reflexes (Achilles tendon reflex loss is one of the earliest detectable signs) with later loss of knee jerks as disease progresses.

Autonomic features including postural dizziness on standing (orthostatic hypotension), increased sweating of the feet, loss of normal heart rate variability, constipation alternating with diarrhea, and erectile dysfunction.
Hand involvement — similar burning and numbness in the fingertips and palms — appearing after years of disease, when the neuropathy has extended proximally.
Muscle wasting (atrophy) of distal leg and foot muscles visible as thinning around the ankles and flattening of the foot arch, developing in advanced axonal loss.
Cutaneous trophic changes including dry, shiny skin on the feet, nail dystrophy, and, in severe cases, painless ulcers in pressure areas reflecting combined sensory and autonomic fiber loss.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild burning or tingling in the tips of the toes that appears after alcohol intake and partly resolves with abstinence — often the first sign of small-fiber involvement, years before clinical weakness.
Loss of the ankle jerk reflex on routine neurological examination in a heavy drinker, even when no symptoms are present — present in up to 50% of asymptomatic heavy drinkers.
Frequent stumbling at night or difficulty walking on uneven surfaces in a chronic drinker, suggesting proprioceptive loss before frank foot-drop.
Orthostatic lightheadedness without cardiac cause in a heavy drinker, signaling early autonomic neuropathy.
emergencySigns: Rapid onset confusion, ophthalmoplegia (eye movement paralysis), and ataxia in a heavy drinker — the triad of Wernicke's encephalopathy, requiring emergency high-dose intravenous thiamine (500 mg three times daily) before any glucose infusion.
Acute severe leg weakness or inability to walk developing over days rather than months — excludes Guillain-Barré syndrome with nerve conduction studies and lumbar puncture.
Syncope or unresponsiveness from autonomic neuropathy causing severe orthostatic hypotension with heart-rate non-variability — requires urgent cardiovascular evaluation.
Signs of alcoholic liver disease decompensation (jaundice, ascites, encephalopathy) coexisting with neuropathy, indicating a systemic nutritional and metabolic emergency.

causes.cause: Direct neurotoxicity of ethanol and its metabolite acetaldehyde
causes.description: Acetaldehyde forms adducts with neurofilament proteins and tubulin, impairing axonal transport and causing oxidative stress within peripheral nerve axons. This mechanism predominates in well-nourished heavy drinkers and produces the slowly progressive painful sensory form of polyneuropathy.

Thiamine is essential for Krebs-cycle enzymes and fatty-acid synthesis in the nerve myelin sheath. Chronic alcohol use reduces intestinal thiamine absorption by up to 50%, impairs hepatic thiamine storage, and increases urinary excretion. Severe deficiency produces the acute-onset Wernicke-overlap form with prominent proprioceptive loss and motor weakness.

Alcohol reduces folate absorption and hepatic storage and causes secondary B12 malabsorption. Folate and B12 deficiencies impair myelin maintenance and nerve regeneration, contributing to demyelinating features and slowing recovery during abstinence.

B6 deficiency from poor nutrition impairs neuronal metabolism; paradoxically, high-dose B6 supplementation above 200 mg/day also causes a predominantly sensory neuropathy — relevant when supplements are taken indiscriminately.

Chronic ethanol metabolism generates reactive oxygen species that deplete antioxidants and impair mitochondrial function within peripheral neurons, particularly dorsal root ganglion cells. This mechanism is additive with thiamine-mediated metabolic failure.

causes.cause: Coexisting liver disease and metabolic toxins
causes.description: Alcoholic liver disease impairs detoxification of endogenous neurotoxins and alters plasma fatty acid composition, providing a second metabolic neuropathic mechanism in patients with advanced hepatic fibrosis or cirrhosis.

riskFactors.factor: Heavy alcohol consumption (>14 drinks/week men, >7 drinks/week women)
riskFactors.description: Risk rises with cumulative lifetime alcohol dose. Studies show a dose-response relationship — patients consuming >100 g ethanol/day (roughly 8 standard drinks) for more than 10 years carry the highest neuropathy burden.

Heavy drinkers who eat poorly have the most severe neuropathy; those with adequate nutrition show predominantly toxic rather than nutritional patterns. Body mass index below 19 doubles neuropathy severity in prospective studies.

Women develop alcoholic neuropathy at lower total alcohol doses and after shorter drinking histories than men, likely from lower alcohol dehydrogenase activity and a higher blood alcohol level per gram of ethanol consumed. The 'telescoping effect' applies to nerve damage as to liver disease.

riskFactors.factor: Genetic variation in alcohol metabolizing enzymes (ADH, ALDH)
riskFactors.description: Variants that increase acetaldehyde accumulation (ALDH2*2 common in East Asian populations) amplify neurotoxicity. ADH1B*2 allele also influences rate of damage.

Diabetes is independently neurotoxic; the combination of diabetic and alcoholic polyneuropathy produces synergistic nerve damage and the most severe clinical presentations, with neuropathy occurring at lower alcohol doses than in normoglycemic drinkers.
The probability of clinical neuropathy rises from approximately 10% at 5 years to over 50% at 20 years of heavy drinking in prospective cohort studies.
Smoking independently reduces peripheral nerve function through microvascular ischemia. Smoking plus heavy drinking carries greater neuropathy risk than either alone in cross-sectional studies.

affectedDemographics: Adults with alcohol use disorder: 25-66% develop clinical neuropathy; subclinical nerve dysfunction detectable by electrophysiology in over 80%.

Women: develop neuropathy at lower cumulative alcohol doses than men due to pharmacokinetic differences; constitute roughly 30-35% of alcoholic neuropathy patients despite being a minority of heavy drinkers.
Middle-aged and older adults (40-65 years): peak clinical presentation after 10-20 years of heavy drinking.
People with concurrent type 2 diabetes: dramatically elevated risk compared with non-diabetic heavy drinkers; combined neuropathy is particularly severe and refractory.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical and electrophysiological. History focuses on average weekly alcohol intake (AUDIT-C questionnaire), years of heavy drinking, dietary quality, and symptom onset pattern. The neurological examination maps sensory deficits — vibration, proprioception, pinprick, temperature — in a length-dependent stocking-glove distribution and documents reflex loss and distal muscle weakness. Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies are the most sensitive diagnostic tools: alcoholic neuropathy characteristically shows reduced amplitude of sural sensory action potentials and peroneal motor action potentials with mild slowing of conduction velocity, reflecting axonal loss rather than demyelination. Small-fiber neuropathy affecting C fibers may show normal standard NCS but is detected by quantitative sensory testing (QST), skin punch biopsy showing reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density, or SUDOSCAN (sudomotor function testing). Blood tests to confirm thiamine, folate, B12, and B6 levels, full blood count (macrocytic anemia suggests B12 or folate deficiency), liver function, and glycated hemoglobin (to exclude diabetic neuropathy) are performed. Nerve biopsy is rarely needed but shows predominantly axonal degeneration with secondary segmental demyelination. The main differential diagnoses are diabetic polyneuropathy, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary), and drug-induced neuropathy — all excluded by history, family history, specific blood tests, and genetic testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy, quantifies severity, and excludes predominant demyelinating disorders
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrodes on the skin measure nerve impulse speed and size in the legs and arms (NCS, 30-45 minutes). Thin needle electrodes in foot and leg muscles record electrical activity at rest and during contraction (EMG). Mild discomfort; results available the same day.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum thiamine, vitamin B12, folate, B6 levels
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies correctable nutritional deficiencies that accelerate neuropathy and guide supplementation dose
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 24-48 hours. Thiamine is best measured as erythrocyte transketolase activity or whole-blood thiamine pyrophosphate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes coexisting diabetic polyneuropathy, which is both common and synergistic in heavy drinkers
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; HbA1c results same day in most laboratories.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin punch biopsy (intraepidermal nerve fiber density)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects small-fiber neuropathy when standard NCS is normal — particularly useful in early or predominantly painful disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3 mm punch biopsy from the lower leg is taken under local anaesthetic in clinic. Results in 1-2 weeks from a specialist neuropathology lab.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardized psychophysical test of vibration and thermal detection thresholds; documents subclinical small-fiber involvement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standardized computer-guided test with vibrating and warming/cooling probes applied to the skin of the feet; takes 30-45 minutes in a specialist clinic.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and full blood count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses coexisting liver disease, macrocytic anemia, and nutritional status that influence management and prognosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results typically available within hours in most healthcare settings.

treatmentOverview: Alcohol abstinence is the only disease-modifying intervention for alcoholic polyneuropathy. Electrophysiological studies show reversal of nerve conduction abnormalities and improvement in symptoms within 6-12 months of complete abstinence in patients without severe axonal loss. Nutritional rehabilitation forms the second pillar: high-dose thiamine replacement (oral thiamine 100-300 mg daily or intravenous thiamine 500 mg three times daily for 5 days in suspected Wernicke's encephalopathy) corrects deficiency-driven components; supplementation with folate 5 mg daily, vitamin B12 1 mg intramuscularly monthly, and a broad multivitamin addresses wider deficiencies including pyridoxine. Management of alcohol use disorder itself — with acamprosate, naltrexone, or disulfiram plus psychosocial support — is an integral component because relapse negates neurological gains. Neuropathic pain is managed with first-line agents: pregabalin (75-300 mg twice daily) or gabapentin (300-1,200 mg three times daily), duloxetine (60-120 mg daily), and low-dose amitriptyline (10-75 mg at night). Topical lidocaine 5% patches or capsaicin 8% patches provide localized pain relief without systemic side effects and are useful when systemic drugs are poorly tolerated. Opioids are avoided given the high addiction risk in this population. Physical therapy prevents contractures, addresses gait instability, and reduces fall risk through balance training. Ankle-foot orthoses manage foot-drop in advanced cases. Regular follow-up electrophysiology at 6-12 month intervals documents recovery and guides treatment adjustment.

medicalTreatments.name: Thiamine (vitamin B1) supplementation (oral 100-300 mg/day; IV 500 mg three times daily for Wernicke's encephalopathy)
medicalTreatments.description: Corrects thiamine deficiency-driven neuropathy and prevents progression to Wernicke's encephalopathy. Oral thiamine is poorly absorbed in active drinkers; parenteral thiamine is preferred when any signs of encephalopathy, ataxia, or ophthalmoplegia are present.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Motor and proprioceptive symptoms most responsive; partial or full reversal in 60-70% of thiamine-deficient patients within 3-6 months (PMID 3202669)

medicalTreatments.name: Pregabalin (75-300 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for neuropathic pain in alcoholic polyneuropathy. Inhibits calcium channel alpha-2-delta subunit, reducing aberrant pain signaling from damaged small fibers. Titrate slowly to minimize sedation and dizziness; note potential for misuse in patients with addiction history.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-50% reduction in pain scores in 35-50% of patients with peripheral neuropathic pain (NICE NG173 2021)

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (60-120 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; preferred over pregabalin in patients with comorbid depression or anxiety, which are highly prevalent in alcohol use disorder. Also addresses insomnia from neuropathic pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50% pain reduction in approximately 40-50% of patients with painful peripheral neuropathy in meta-analyses (PMID 24385423)

medicalTreatments.name: Amitriptyline (10-75 mg at night)
medicalTreatments.description: Tricyclic antidepressant with established neuropathic pain efficacy. Useful adjunct when sleep disruption is prominent. Start at 10 mg and titrate weekly; avoid in patients with cardiac arrhythmia or alcoholic cardiomyopathy, and use with caution in those at risk of falls.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT ~3.6 for 50% pain relief in peripheral neuropathy (Cochrane review 2015, PMID 26213910)

medicalTreatments.name: Naltrexone (50 mg daily) or acamprosate (666 mg three times daily) for alcohol use disorder
medicalTreatments.description: Pharmacotherapy for alcohol use disorder is an integral part of managing alcoholic polyneuropathy since sustained abstinence is the primary neuroprotective intervention. Naltrexone reduces craving and relapse; acamprosate reduces protracted withdrawal. Neither directly treats the neuropathy but prevents further nerve damage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Naltrexone reduces heavy drinking days by approximately 25% over 12 weeks versus placebo in meta-analyses (PMID 16182984)

medicalTreatments.name: Topical capsaicin 8% patch (high-concentration)
medicalTreatments.description: A single 60-minute patch application desensitizes cutaneous nociceptors via TRPV1 agonist-induced degeneration of small-fiber nerve terminals. Provides 8-12 weeks of pain relief without systemic effects, suited for patients who cannot tolerate oral agents.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30% pain reduction versus 1% vehicle patch in painful peripheral neuropathy in phase 3 trials (PMID 21134501)

alternativeTreatments.name: Alpha-lipoic acid (600 mg daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Antioxidant with evidence for reducing neuropathic pain and improving nerve function in diabetic neuropathy; mechanism (glutathione regeneration and mitochondrial support) relevant to alcoholic neuropathy. A small RCT (PMID 28049282) showed improved sensory symptoms in alcoholic neuropathy with 600 mg oral daily for 3 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Adjunct pain modulation for distal burning symptoms; acts via gate-control mechanism. Patient-operated device for home use with low adverse-effect profile.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurologist should confirm the diagnosis, order appropriate electrophysiological tests, and exclude other causes of polyneuropathy (diabetic, hereditary, toxic). An addiction medicine specialist or psychiatrist manages the underlying alcohol use disorder, which is the primary modifiable driver of the neuropathy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a neurologist with expertise in peripheral nerve and neuromuscular disease for diagnosis and monitoring. For ongoing management, a team including an addiction medicine specialist, dietitian, and physiotherapist produces better long-term outcomes than a single clinician.
hospitalCriteria: EMG and nerve conduction study capability in the neurology department
Intravenous thiamine stock for acute Wernicke-overlap cases
Addiction medicine or liaison psychiatry service for alcohol use disorder management
Physiotherapy and occupational therapy for gait rehabilitation and falls prevention
Dietitian experienced in alcohol-related nutritional deficiencies
keyFacilities: Community rehabilitation and occupational therapy services
preventionStrategies: Reduce alcohol consumption to within national guidelines (UK: ≤14 units/week; US: ≤14 drinks/week for men, ≤7 for women) — dose-response evidence makes even partial reduction protective.
Ensure adequate thiamine intake through diet or supplementation — thiamine 100 mg/day is recommended for all heavy drinkers regardless of symptoms.
Maintain regular balanced diet with adequate B-vitamin sources (legumes, meat, dairy, dark leafy greens) to prevent compounding nutritional neuropathy.
Seek treatment for alcohol use disorder early through primary care or addiction services — neurological damage begins silently and accumulates over years before symptoms appear.
Control blood glucose if diabetic — diabetic neuropathy is synergistically more damaging than either condition alone.
lifestyleModifications: Complete abstinence from alcohol is the only intervention proven to halt and partially reverse nerve damage — even significant reduction short of abstinence produces measurable neurological improvement.
Take prescribed B-vitamin supplements consistently; do not skip doses as thiamine stores deplete within 18 days without dietary intake.
Perform supervised balance and gait exercises daily — fall prevention is critical as proprioceptive loss increases fracture risk substantially.
Use protective footwear and check feet daily for cuts, blisters, or pressure areas that may go unnoticed due to sensory loss.
Avoid hot-water bottles or heated blankets on insensate feet — thermal burns are a recognized complication of foot insensitivity.
Attend regular neurology follow-up — nerve conduction studies at 6-12 months document recovery and guide treatment adjustments.

recommended: Thiamine-rich foods (whole grains, pork, legumes, nuts) or daily thiamine supplementation of 100-300 mg
Vitamin B12 sources (red meat, fish, eggs, dairy) or monthly B12 injections if absorptive deficiency is confirmed
Folate-rich foods (leafy green vegetables, legumes, fortified cereals) to support myelin maintenance
Protein-rich diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to prevent muscle wasting from disuse and poor nutrition
avoid: All alcoholic beverages — even low-volume consumption sustains neurotoxic acetaldehyde exposure
High-dose vitamin B6 supplements above 100 mg/day, which cause their own toxic sensory neuropathy
Processed foods displacing nutrient-dense options in the diet, worsening overall nutritional neuropathy risk

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic exercise (walking, cycling) 30 minutes daily maintains cardiovascular health and reduces neuropathic pain intensity through endorphin release. Balance training (standing on one foot, heel-to-toe walking, yoga) twice daily reduces fall risk in patients with proprioceptive loss. Water-based exercise (aquatic physiotherapy) reduces fall risk and is well-tolerated in patients with foot pain.
dailyManagement: Take thiamine and B-vitamin supplements with breakfast every day without exception.
Inspect both feet every evening for cuts, blisters, or pressure sores — do not rely on pain sensation.
Take prescribed neuropathic pain medication at consistent times to maintain steady plasma levels.
Perform prescribed balance and ankle-strengthening exercises in the morning when fatigue is lowest.
Record alcohol-free days in a diary or app linked to the addiction medicine follow-up plan.
Keep follow-up appointments with neurology, addiction medicine, and dietitian at 3-6 month intervals.
prognosis: Prognosis depends heavily on abstinence and the degree of axonal loss at diagnosis. Patients who achieve complete abstinence and adequate nutritional replacement experience measurable electrophysiological improvement in nerve conduction studies within 6-12 months and clinical improvement in pain and balance within 3-6 months. Recovery of sensory function is more consistent than recovery of motor function; severe axonal loss with established foot-drop rarely fully reverses. Among patients who continue drinking, neuropathy progresses relentlessly — each additional year of heavy consumption adds cumulative axonal loss that becomes irreversible. Studies show that roughly 60-70% of abstinent patients improve subjective symptoms over 2 years, while fewer than 20% of those who continue drinking show any improvement. Autonomic neuropathy, particularly cardiac autonomic dysfunction, carries independent cardiovascular mortality risk even after abstinence; sudden cardiac death is more common in patients with alcoholic cardiac autonomic neuropathy.
recoveryTimeline: Pain improvement: 3-6 months of abstinence plus supplementation. Sensory recovery: 6-18 months. Motor recovery (ankle dorsiflexion): 12-24 months if axonal loss is moderate. Electrophysiological normalization: 1-3 years with complete abstinence in early-stage disease. Severe axonal loss (amplitude below 10% of normal on sural nerve NCS) rarely fully normalizes.
complications: Foot-drop and gait instability leading to falls and fractures — present in 15-20% of patients with long-standing disease; managed with ankle-foot orthoses and physiotherapy.
Chronic neuropathic pain refractory to pharmacotherapy, contributing to depression, sleep disruption, and reduced quality of life in 30-40% of severe cases.
Cardiac autonomic neuropathy with impaired heart-rate variability and QTc prolongation, increasing sudden cardiac death risk — detected by 24-hour Holter monitoring.
Painless foot ulceration from combined sensory, autonomic, and vascular compromise in severe polyneuropathy, risking secondary infection and osteomyelitis.
Wernicke's encephalopathy progression to Korsakoff psychosis if thiamine deficiency is not treated promptly — Korsakoff amnesia is largely irreversible.
Depression and suicidality: rates of major depression exceed 50% in patients with alcohol use disorder and chronic painful neuropathy — requires active screening and treatment.

supportResources.name: Alcoholics Anonymous (AA)
supportResources.description: Peer-support 12-step programme for abstinence from alcohol with global network of meetings; primary tool for long-term sobriety maintenance.

supportResources.name: National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism (NIAAA)
supportResources.description: US federal resource with treatment finder, patient guides on alcohol-related health complications, and clinical trial information.

supportResources.name: Peripheral Neuropathy UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information and peer support for people living with peripheral neuropathy of all causes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetic Polyneuropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetic neuropathy produces a clinically identical length-dependent painful sensorimotor neuropathy. The two are distinguished by fasting glucose and HbA1c, clinical history, and can coexist (synergistically worsening outcomes). An HbA1c above 48 mmol/mol confirms diabetes as a contributing cause even in heavy drinkers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Idiopathic Small Fiber Neuropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Idiopathic small-fiber neuropathy causes isolated burning pain and autonomic features with normal standard nerve conduction studies and no alcohol history. Skin biopsy showing reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density is identical in both. History of heavy drinking and deficient B-vitamin levels distinguish alcoholic from idiopathic cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS causes sensory symptoms and weakness through central demyelination, with brisk or hyperreflexic tendon reflexes, upper motor neuron signs, and white-matter lesions on brain MRI. Alcoholic neuropathy produces flaccid hyporeflexic weakness and shows peripheral nerve abnormalities on EMG without central lesions.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Neuropathic pain is the dominant symptomatic burden in alcoholic polyneuropathy, driving pharmacological management and quality-of-life impact.

relatedConditions.name: Alcohol Use Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Alcohol use disorder is the direct underlying cause and its treatment — pharmacological and behavioral — is the primary intervention for preventing neuropathy progression.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic painful alcoholic polyneuropathy fulfills criteria for chronic pain syndrome and requires multidisciplinary management including pain psychology.

relatedConditions.name: Autonomic Dysreflexia
relatedConditions.relevance: Autonomic dysfunction including orthostatic hypotension, cardiac arrhythmia, and sweating abnormalities is a recognized feature of severe alcoholic polyneuropathy affecting small autonomic fibers.

faqs.question: What is alcoholic polyneuropathy?
faqs.answer: Alcoholic polyneuropathy is nerve damage caused by chronic heavy alcohol use. Ethanol and its toxic metabolite acetaldehyde directly damage peripheral nerve axons, compounded by B-vitamin deficiencies — especially thiamine — that impair nerve maintenance. It typically causes burning foot pain, numbness in the feet and legs, and balance problems.

faqs.question: Can alcoholic neuropathy be reversed?
faqs.answer: Yes, partially. Complete abstinence from alcohol plus B-vitamin supplementation allows nerve recovery in 60-70% of patients, with pain and sensory improvement over 6-18 months. Severe established axonal loss — especially foot-drop — rarely reverses fully. Recovery is much better in early disease and with permanent abstinence.

faqs.question: How much alcohol causes neuropathy?
faqs.answer: Risk rises sharply above 100 g of pure ethanol daily (about 8 standard drinks) sustained over 10 or more years. Women develop neuropathy at lower doses due to pharmacokinetic differences. Subclinical nerve damage on electrophysiology is detectable in over 80% of heavy drinkers regardless of symptoms.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of alcoholic neuropathy?
faqs.answer: The earliest symptoms are burning or tingling in the tips of the toes, often worse at night. Mild unsteadiness in the dark and loss of the ankle reflex on examination may precede subjective symptoms. These signs can appear silently and progress for years before the patient seeks medical attention.

faqs.question: How is alcoholic neuropathy treated?
faqs.answer: The primary treatment is alcohol abstinence, which is the only proven disease-modifying intervention. Thiamine (100-300 mg/day), B12, and folate supplementation correct nutritional deficits. Neuropathic pain is managed with pregabalin, duloxetine, or amitriptyline. Physical therapy reduces fall risk and maintains mobility.

faqs.question: What vitamins help alcoholic neuropathy?
faqs.answer: Thiamine (vitamin B1) is the most critical — it corrects deficiency-driven axonal damage and prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy. Folate, vitamin B12, and pyridoxine (B6) should also be replaced. A daily multivitamin plus oral thiamine 100-300 mg is the standard minimum for any patient with alcohol use disorder.

faqs.question: How long does alcoholic neuropathy take to develop?
faqs.answer: Clinical symptoms typically emerge after 10-20 years of heavy drinking, though subclinical nerve abnormalities appear on electrophysiology much earlier. Women and people with concurrent diabetes may develop symptoms after shorter exposure durations because of synergistic risk factors.

faqs.question: Is alcoholic neuropathy painful?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most cases. Burning, aching, or electric pain in the feet is the dominant symptom. Allodynia — pain from light touch such as bedsheets — affects many patients at night. Severe axonal loss can paradoxically produce numbness rather than pain, often accompanied by loss of protective sensation.

faqs.question: Does alcoholic neuropathy affect the hands?
faqs.answer: Yes, eventually. Alcoholic polyneuropathy is length-dependent, affecting the longest nerves first. Symptoms begin in the feet and ascend to the ankles and legs before appearing in the fingertips and hands, typically after years of progression. Hand symptoms indicate more advanced and widespread nerve damage.

faqs.question: What is the difference between alcoholic and diabetic neuropathy?
faqs.answer: Both cause identical length-dependent painful sensorimotor polyneuropathy. The distinction relies on clinical history, HbA1c, and fasting glucose. Both can coexist in the same patient, producing worse combined nerve damage. Treatment differs: alcoholic neuropathy requires abstinence and B-vitamins; diabetic neuropathy requires glucose control.

faqs.question: Can alcoholic neuropathy cause foot-drop?
faqs.answer: Yes. Advanced alcoholic polyneuropathy can weaken the peroneal nerve-innervated ankle dorsiflexors, causing foot-drop — an inability to lift the foot when walking, producing a steppage gait. Ankle-foot orthoses correct the gait abnormality; partial recovery of dorsiflexion occurs with abstinence in some patients.

faqs.question: Is alcoholic neuropathy the same as Wernicke's encephalopathy?
faqs.answer: They are related but distinct. Wernicke's encephalopathy is acute brain damage from severe thiamine deficiency, causing confusion, eye movement abnormalities, and ataxia — a medical emergency. Alcoholic polyneuropathy is peripheral nerve damage from combined ethanol toxicity and B-vitamin deficiency. Both are more common together than separately.

faqs.question: What tests diagnose alcoholic neuropathy?
faqs.answer: Nerve conduction studies and EMG confirm axonal polyneuropathy and grade severity. Blood tests assess thiamine, B12, folate, HbA1c, and liver function. Skin biopsy measuring intraepidermal nerve fiber density diagnoses small-fiber neuropathy when standard NCS is normal. Nerve biopsy is rarely necessary.

faqs.question: Can you walk normally with alcoholic neuropathy?
faqs.answer: In mild to moderate disease, walking is preserved with some unsteadiness in the dark from proprioceptive loss. Advanced neuropathy with significant ankle weakness or foot-drop impairs walking and increases fall risk. Physiotherapy, balance training, and ankle-foot orthoses maintain safe independent walking in most patients.

faqs.question: Does stopping alcohol improve neuropathy immediately?
faqs.answer: Not immediately, but abstinence is the only intervention proven to produce meaningful long-term recovery. Pain improvement typically begins within 3-6 months of stopping alcohol. Sensory and electrophysiological recovery follows over 6-18 months. Motor deficits — especially foot-drop — recover more slowly and incompletely.

metaDescription: Alcoholic polyneuropathy causes burning foot pain, numbness, and weakness from alcohol-induced nerve damage. Diagnosis, B-vitamin treatment, and recovery guide.

sources.title: Chopra K, Tiwari V. 2012. Alcoholic neuropathy: possible mechanisms and future treatment possibilities. British Journal of Clinical Pharmacology 73(3):348-62.
Victor M, Adams RD, Collins GH. 1971. The Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome. Contemporary Neurology Series 7:1-206.
Koike H, Sobue G. 2006. Alcoholic neuropathy. Current Opinion in Neurology 19(5):481-6.
Balakrishnan S, Bhatt SP, Srinivasan K. 2006. Thiamine and the nervous system: an overview of the metabolism of thiamine. Current Enzyme Inhibition 2(1):151-65.
Finnerup NB, Attal N, Haroutounian S, et al. 2015. Pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet Neurology 14(2):162-73.
NICE Clinical Guideline NG173. 2021. Neuropathic pain in adults: pharmacological management in non-specialist settings. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, London.
Mason JB, Choi SW. 2005. Effects of alcohol on folate metabolism: implications for carcinogenesis. Alcohol 35(3):235-41.
